does: This paper investigates yield—the rate at which LLM-based multi-agent systems flip from correct to incorrect answers under simulated peer disagreement. Rather than attributing this vulnerability to RLHF-induced sycophancy as commonly believed, the authors conduct systematic testing across four model families and use activation patching to localize the corruption mechanism. They discover that pretrained base models actually exhibit higher yield rates than their Instruct variants, contradicting the sycophancy hypothesis. Through fine-grained intervention experiments, they show the corruption localizes to a narrow mid-layer window where attention carries causal weight while MLP contribution is minimal. Activation-space analysis reveals that pressure suppresses clean-reasoning features rather than activating a distinct sycophancy circuit, with the vulnerability decomposing into two independent factors—channel framing and consensus strength—whose interaction produces a 47.5 percentage-point yield gap at majority consensus.
connects: This work builds on growing literature examining failures in multi-agent LLM systems and the mechanistic understanding of alignment techniques like RLHF. It extends prior work investigating whether alignment training introduces new failure modes by conducting controlled experiments with both base and instruction-tuned models. The paper connects to broader mechanistic interpretability research on attention and MLP contributions in transformer reasoning, building on techniques like activation patching developed in prior neuroscience-inspired studies of neural networks. It also engages with emerging work on adversarial pressure and conformity in language models, challenging the dominant attribution of multi-agent failures to RLHF-specific sycophancy behaviors and instead pointing toward more fundamental reasoning suppression mechanisms shared across model types.
enables: This work opens directions for developing targeted mitigations beyond alignment training, since alignment alone cannot fix a vulnerability rooted in fundamental reasoning suppression rather than learned sycophantic behaviors. Understanding that pressure suppresses clean-reasoning features rather than activating new circuits suggests interventions could restore robust reasoning under disagreement through feature-space manipulation or architectural modifications. The decomposition of yield into channel framing and consensus strength factors enables future work to design better multi-agent architectures or prompting strategies that separately address these components. The finding that a single correctly-arguing dissenter substantially reduces yield points toward practical ensemble and debate strategies. This research also sets up investigations into whether similar suppression mechanisms underlie other multi-agent failures, and whether training methods targeting feature preservation under pressure could produce models more resilient to social conformity effects in collaborative reasoning tasks.
